fault_hypothesis: On line 62, the NaN check uses `loss_value == loss_value` as the NaN guard, but this is placed as the FIRST condition with AND, so when loss_value is NaN, `loss_value == loss_value` is False and the whole condition short-circuits to False, skipping the return False branch. The condition should instead return False when loss_value is NaN (i.e., use `not (loss_value == loss_value)` as a separate check, or use `math.isnan`).

spec.expr: not (loss_value != loss_value) or (loss_value == loss_value and loss_value > max_loss)

constraints.intent: When loss_value is NaN, the condition on line 62 should evaluate to True so the function returns False; this checks that NaN losses are caught by the guard.
After the if-branch is not taken, loss_value must be non-NaN and within max_loss bounds; a NaN value here indicates the guard failed to catch it.
Confirm the NaN identity property holds before the guard condition is evaluated, ensuring the check logic is correct.
If loss_value is NaN, the function must return False; a True return for NaN input is the observed bug symptom.
